claims_tested: R-EFF001: AST analysis catches >= 80% of string concatenation evasion techniques
R-EFF002: AST analysis produces minimal false positives on safe code patterns (1 known: very-long-string-concat)

string-construction.description: Novel string construction methods: format(), f-strings, bytes.decode(), base64, hex, slicing, replace, etc.

indirect-calls.description: Indirect call patterns: aliasing, nested getattr, vars(), globals(), __dict__, functools.reduce

obfuscation-layers.description: Multiple indirection levels: chr+getattr+call, join+getattr, arithmetic in chr(), lambda

os-subprocess-evasion.description: OS/subprocess variants: os.popen, os.execv, os.spawnl, shell via variable, **kwargs

deserialization-evasion.description: Deserialization variants: shelve, yaml.unsafe_load, cloudpickle, dill, from-import pickle

false-positives.description: Safe code patterns that should NOT trigger findings

edge-cases.description: Edge cases: empty input, syntax errors, deep nesting, unicode homoglyphs, decorators

techniques_used: Variable aliasing (assign eval to var, import-as)
Deep call chains (nested getattr, multi-level attribute)
Arithmetic obfuscation (chr with expressions, not constants)
Syntax abuse (decorator, walrus operator, lambda wrapper)
Resource exhaustion (very long concat chains, deep nesting)
